themeBlurb: Tenses, subject-verb agreement, articles, prepositions, pronouns. Drilled across Paper 2 Booklet A (Grammar MCQ) and Booklet B (Grammar Cloze and Editing).

grammar.title: Grammar Foundations
grammar.blurb: The six grammar areas the PSLE tests: tense, subject-verb agreement, articles, prepositions, pronouns, conjunctions.
keyQuestions: Which tense does the sentence need: simple past, past continuous, or past perfect?
Does the verb match a singular or plural subject?
Which preposition naturally follows this verb?

glossary.definition: The form of a verb that shows when an action takes place (past, present, future).
glossary.template: She _____ to school every morning. (walks / walked)

glossary.definition: A singular subject takes a singular verb; a plural subject takes a plural verb.
Words 'a', 'an', and 'the' used before nouns. 'A/an' for non-specific; 'the' for specific. The choice between 'a' and 'an' depends on the SOUND that follows, not the letter.
glossary.template: She is _____ honest person. (a / an — 'an' because honest starts with a vowel sound, even though the letter is a consonant.)

glossary.definition: Words like 'in', 'on', 'at', 'with', 'about' that show relationships between other words.
The seven coordinating conjunctions: For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So.
glossary.template: Memorise the mnemonic — these are the joiners that take a comma before them when linking two independent clauses.

glossary.definition: To check SVA, put any phrase between subject and verb in mental brackets, then test only the subject.
glossary.template: The box [of chocolates] _____ on the table. (is — subject is 'box', not 'chocolates'.)

grammar.learnHTML:
Why grammar matters

Grammar is tested across Paper 2 Booklet A (Grammar MCQ, 10 marks) and Booklet B (Grammar Cloze 10 marks + roughly half of the 10-mark Editing component). At least 25 marks ride on grammar precision. A small grammar gap costs more here than anywhere else in the paper.

The six grammar areas (the order matters when checking)

  1. Tense — simple present, present continuous, simple past, past continuous, present perfect, past perfect, future.
  2. Subject-Verb Agreement (SVA) — singular subjects take singular verbs (The boy walks); plural subjects take plural verbs (The boys walk).
  3. Articles — 'a' / 'an' for non-specific; 'the' for specific. Use 'an' before vowel sounds: an hour, a university, an honest person.
  4. Prepositions — verb-preposition pairs are fixed: depend on, rely on, succeed in, agree with.
  5. Pronouns — subject (I, he, she, we, they), object (me, him, her, us, them), possessive (my, his, her, our, their).
  6. Conjunctions — joining words: and, but, or, because, although, while, since. FANBOYS = For/And/Nor/But/Or/Yet/So.

Tense decision tree (the four questions)

  1. Is the action completed? → Simple past. ("She walked to school yesterday.")
  2. Did one past action happen BEFORE another past action? → Past perfect (had + past participle) for the earlier one. ("By the time the bell rang, the students had already finished their lunch.")
  3. Is the action happening right now? → Present continuous (am/is/are + -ing). ("She is walking to school.")
  4. Is the action a habit or routine? → Simple present. ("She walks to school every morning.")

Subject-Verb Agreement traps (the high-frequency ones)

  • Each / Every + singular: Each of the boys IS here. (NOT "are" — Each is singular.)
  • Either / Neither + singular: Neither of them WAS on time.
  • One of + singular: One of the books IS missing.
  • Collective nouns (Singapore usage) + singular: The team IS celebrating. / The class HAS finished its homework.
  • Phrases between subject and verb — ignore them (bracket rule): The box [of chocolates] IS on the table. Subject is "box", not "chocolates".
  • Indefinite pronouns (everyone, anyone, somebody) + singular: Everyone WAS happy.

Articles — the 'vowel sound' rule

Choose 'a' or 'an' by the SOUND that follows, not by the letter.

WordFirst soundArticle
hourOW (silent h)an hour
honestON (silent h)an honest person
universityYOO (consonant sound)a university
EuropeanYOOa European
umbrellaUM (vowel sound)an umbrella

Pronoun types (and the most-tested trap)

  • Subject: I, you, he, she, it, we, they
  • Object: me, you, him, her, it, us, them
  • Possessive adjective: my, your, his, her, its, our, their (+ noun)
  • Possessive pronoun: mine, yours, his, hers, ours, theirs (stands alone)
  • Reflexive: myself, yourself, himself, herself, itself, ourselves, themselves
The most-tested trap: after a preposition, use the OBJECT pronoun. "Between you and ME" (NOT "Between you and I"). "He gave it to him and ME" (NOT "to him and I").

Verb-preposition pairs to memorise (the 16 highest-frequency)

depend ON · rely ON · insist ON · agree WITH (a person) · agree TO (a plan) · apologise FOR · complain ABOUT · worry ABOUT · listen TO · wait FOR · belong TO · consist OF · suffer FROM · benefit FROM · participate IN · succeed IN · believe IN · interfere WITH · accuse OF · blame FOR · congratulate ON · thank FOR · responsible FOR · interested IN · good AT · afraid OF
grammar.summaryHTML: Grammar in the PSLE is tested across Paper 2 Booklet A (Grammar MCQ, 10 marks) and Booklet B (Grammar Cloze 10 marks + half of the 10-mark Editing). Total: at least 25 marks. The full reference is Grammar_Complete_Reference.md.
pslePrompts: Always identify the tense BEFORE filling the blank.
Re-read the full sentence after filling it — does it flow?
Watch for the 'a/an' trap with silent letters: 'an hour', 'a university', 'an honest person'.
Memorise the high-frequency verb-preposition pairs as units, not separately.
After a preposition, use the OBJECT pronoun: 'between you and me'.

commonTraps.wrong: "By the time the bell rang, the students have finished their lunch."
commonTraps.fix: Use past perfect for the earlier of two past events: "…the students HAD finished their lunch."
commonTraps.why: 'By the time' signals two past events; the earlier one needs past perfect (had + past participle).

commonTraps.wrong: "Each of the boys are here." / "Neither of them were on time."
commonTraps.fix: Each / Neither / Either / One of are SINGULAR. "Each of the boys IS here."
commonTraps.why: The pronoun (Each, Neither) is the grammatical subject — singular — regardless of the plural noun that follows.

commonTraps.wrong: "She is a honest person." / "He is an university student."
commonTraps.fix: Apply the SOUND rule: "an honest" (h is silent, vowel sound), "a university" (yoo, consonant sound).
commonTraps.why: Articles depend on the SOUND that follows, not the letter. 'h' can be silent; 'u' can sound like 'yoo'.

commonTraps.wrong: "Between you and I, this is hard." / "He gave it to John and I."
commonTraps.fix: After a preposition, use the OBJECT pronoun: "between you and ME", "to John and ME".
commonTraps.why: Prepositions (between, to, for, with) demand object pronouns. "I" is a subject pronoun and never follows a preposition.

commonTraps.wrong: "She succeeded to her goal." / "He interfered to my plan."
commonTraps.fix: Memorise the verb-preposition pair: succeed IN, interfere WITH, depend ON, agree WITH.
commonTraps.why: These pairs are fixed — there's no rule, only memorisation. Drill the 16 highest-frequency pairs to automaticity.

commonTraps.wrong: "If I was you, I would stay."
commonTraps.fix: After hypothetical 'If', use the subjunctive WERE: "If I WERE you, I would stay."
commonTraps.why: Hypothetical or contrary-to-fact statements use 'were' for all persons (I were, he were, they were). This is the second-conditional rule.

masteryCheckpoints: You can identify the tense of any sentence and justify it using the four-question decision tree.
You apply the bracket rule for SVA: find the subject, mental-bracket the phrase in the middle, match the verb to the subject only.
You know the 16 highest-frequency verb-preposition pairs by heart (depend on, agree with, succeed in, etc.).
You apply the SOUND rule for a/an with at least three examples (an hour, a university, an honest person).
After any preposition, you use the OBJECT pronoun (me, him, us, them) — never the subject pronoun (I, he, we, they).
You can spell out FANBOYS (For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So) and use a comma before them when joining two independent clauses.

questions.question: By the time the bell rang, the students _____ their work.
questions.explanation: Past perfect 'had finished' is correct because the finishing happened BEFORE another past event (the bell ringing). 'By the time' is a strong signal for past perfect.

questions.question: Neither of the boys _____ available to help.
questions.explanation: 'Neither of' takes a singular verb. 'Neither' is grammatically singular, regardless of the plural noun that follows. So 'was' is correct.

questions.question: She succeeded _____ persuading her parents to let her join the camp.
questions.explanation: The verb 'succeed' takes the preposition 'in'. Other verb-preposition pairs to memorise: depend on, rely on, agree with, apologise for, insist on.

questions.question: Between you and _____, this assignment is harder than I expected.
questions.explanation: After a preposition (between, to, for, with), use the OBJECT pronoun: me, him, her, us, them. 'Between you and I' is one of the most common pronoun errors at PSLE level.

questions.question: If I _____ in your position, I would speak to the teacher.
questions.explanation: Hypothetical 'If' statements use the subjunctive WERE for all persons (I were, he were, they were). 'If I were you…' is the standard PSLE second-conditional pattern.
